serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of canh bầu tôm bằm is approximately 400 g, providing around 180 kcal, 18 g protein, 6 g fat, and 12 g carbohydrate per serving.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Use in East Asia and Southeast Asia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for canh bầu tôm bằm exists in FAO/INFOODS SEA or HPB Singapore databases. Values are estimated by component analysis: bottle gourd (bầu) nutrient profile from FAO/INFOODS SEA table, shrimp (minced) from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15152 — shrimp, mixed species, raw), fish sauce contribution for sodium, and light broth dilution factor applied. Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against these component estimates and found to be broadly consistent; retained with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 (Atkinson, Foster-Powell, Brand-Miller)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for canh bầu tôm bằm. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate contributor being bottle gourd / winter melon, a non-starchy vegetable with negligible starch content and high water content. Analogous reference: mixed vegetable soup with low-starch vegetables, GI ~35 per University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (35 × 12 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 4.2 (very low).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 212.5 mg/100 g from fish sauce and broth; 850 mg per 400 g serving
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and low-sodium broth to bring sodium below 200 mg/100 g, and limit portion size to keep total sodium from this dish under 600 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (3.0 g/100 g), low sugar (1.0 g/100 g), low estimated GI (~35), high water content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Canh bầu tôm bằm is suitable for people with type 2 diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate and sugar content, low glycemic index, and high satiety from protein and water volume; avoid adding sugar to the broth and monitor fish sauce sodium.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.5 g/100 g), zero trans fat, lean shrimp protein, moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is low in saturated fat and contains no trans fat, making it broadly suitable for cardiovascular health; however, sodium at 212.5 mg/100 g (850 mg per bowl) warrants portion control and fish sauce reduction for patients on sodium-restricted cardiac diets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low saturated fat, shrimp omega-3 content, anti-inflammatory aromatics (garlic, black pepper)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The shrimp-only version of this soup is appropriate for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its low saturated fat, absence of trans fat, and shrimp-derived omega-3 fatty acids; request no added pork mince to avoid arachidonic acid (omega-6) amplification of inflammatory pathways, and reduce fish sauce to limit sodium-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Canh bầu tôm bằm (squash soup with minced shrimp) is a traditional Vietnamese clear soup made by simmering tender slices of bottle gourd (bầu, also called Vietnamese squash or opo squash) with seasoned minced shrimp in a light broth flavoured with fish sauce, shallots, spring onion, and black pepper. The dish is a staple of everyday Vietnamese home cooking, valued for its delicate, savoury flavour, soft vegetable texture, and nourishing simplicity. The bottle gourd contributes a mild, slightly sweet flavour and absorbs the umami-rich broth, while the minced shrimp provides lean protein and a subtle seafood sweetness. Nutritionally, the soup is low in calories (~45 kcal/100 g), low in carbohydrates (3.0 g/100 g), and provides moderate protein (4.5 g/100 g) with minimal saturated fat (0.5 g/100 g) and no trans fat. Sodium is moderate at 212.5 mg/100 g, primarily from fish sauce, which is a key consideration for hypertension and cardiac patients. The glycemic index is estimated low (~35) due to the non-starchy vegetable base and high water content, making it suitable for type 2 diabetes management. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. It is commonly served as part of a multi-dish Vietnamese family meal alongside steamed rice, stir-fried vegetables, and a protein dish.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for canh bầu tôm bằm exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore, FAO/INFOODS SEA, USDA). Nutrient values are estimated by component analysis using FAO/INFOODS SEA (bottle gourd) and USDA SR Legacy (shrimp), cross-checked against the input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7). Values are internally consistent and plausible but cannot be verified against a direct published measurement.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for this Vietnamese soup preparation is not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this specific dish preparation is not reported in available approved sources; estimated ~180 mg/100 g from components but not confirmed. 3. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this composite soup dish is not directly reported in approved sources. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: micronutrient data for this composite Vietnamese soup dish is not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central as a prepared dish entry. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for this minimally processed seafood and vegetable soup with no hydrogenated fats. 6. GI is an analogous estimate (~35, low) based on the non-starchy vegetable and seafood composition; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. Input record provenance was ai_generated with confidence 0.7 from grabfood; values have been cross-validated against component-level approved sources and retained. 8. country_of_origin in input was listed as Singapore; this has been corrected to Vietnam as the dish is a traditional Vietnamese preparation; it may be served in Singapore Vietnamese restaurants.
